title: Swedish Forces Jam Suspected Russian Drone Near French Carrier Charles De Gaulle
description: Swedish forces intercepted and jammed a suspected Russian drone Wednesday in the Öresund Strait near the port city of Malmo where French aircraft carrier FS Charles De Gaulle (R91) is docked. text:
Swedish forces intercepted and jammed a suspected Russian drone Wednesday in the Öresund Strait near the port city of Malmo where French aircraft carrier FS Charles De Gaulle (R91) is docked.
A drone took off from a nearby Russian ship and approached the French aircraft carrier – in port for a visit with Swedish and French officials – before the drone was discovered by a Swedish Navy patrol vessel, the Swedish Armed Forces said Friday. The suspicious drone was observed taking off from Russian signals intelligence vessel Zhigulevsk in Öresund.
The Swedish Armed Forces said its Navy “acted swiftly to jam the drone.” Contact with the drone was subsequently lost, and no further drone observations have been made, the news release reads.
Swedish Armed Forces are now investigating whether the incident constitutes a violation of the Access Ordinance. The Access Ordinance requires foreign state aircraft, military helicopters, non-commercial flights and naval vessels to obtain permission to enter Swedish waters and airspace.
Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov told Moscow media the Swedish claims were absurd, but was unable to provide additional information.
French military spokesperson Guillaume Vernet told The Associated Press on Friday that the drone was detected 10 kilometers from Charles de Gaulle.
Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson and Defense Minister Pål Jonson visited the carrier Friday, accompanied by French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot, Swedish Foreign Minister Maria Stenergard and French Deputy Defense Minister Alice Rufo.
“This impressive aircraft carrier supports NATO’s deterrence and defense activities. France and Sweden contribute strongly to European security, growth and competitiveness. Together, we stand up for Ukraine, for a rules-based world order and for Europe,” Kristersson wrote on social media platform X after the visit.
In a press conference on the carrier, the French Foreign Minister Barrot described the incident as “a ridiculous provocation.”
“If indeed as the [Swedish] Minister of Defense suggested, there is a potential Russian origin for this incident. The only conclusion I would draw is that it would be a ridiculous provocation,” Barrot said.
Charles De Gaulle and Royal Netherlands Navy frigate HNLMS Evertsen (F805), which is integrated into the French Carrier Strike Group (CSG), docked Tuesday in Malmo. This is the first time Charles de Gaulle and its aircraft carrier battle group have visited Sweden. Previously, Sweden was visited by American amphibious assault ship USS Kearsarge (LHD-3) in 2022 and the British aircraft carrier HMS Queen Elizabeth (R08) in 2023.
Other components of the French CSG docked in Copenhagen, Denmark. The French CSG is currently on the first leg of its Mission Lafayette 26 deployment, which is taking the strike group to the Baltic and North seas.
The proliferation of drones is of increasing concern to most militaries as they can be used for illegal surveillance or converted to carry out attacks. Especially vulnerable is civilian infrastructure, which does not have inherent defense capabilities.
The Royal Navy’s latest execution of its Sharpshooter training program – which prepares warships and their embarked helicopters in defending against simultaneous drone, missile and air attacks – saw RN destroyer HMS Duncan (D37) and its embarked Wildcat helicopter from 815 Naval Air Squadron conduct a training scenario off the coast of Wales meant to train in defending critical national infrastructure.
In the scenario at Aberporth Range in Cardigan Bay, the destroyer and helicopter faced attacks from swarms of surface and aerial drones, as well as virtual missiles, for 72 hours. The training – codenamed Sharpshooter – is designed to replicate real-life operations such as the conditions faced by HMS Diamond (D34) in the Red Sea as the Houthis fired drones and missiles from Yemen.
The warfighting scenario placed the destroyer within a notional task group protecting critical national infrastructure where it had to restore maritime security in a contested region. The training was delivered by the RN’s specialist Fleet Operational Standards and Training teams and defense firms QinetiQ and Inzpire.
Opposing units were Hammerhead Uncrewed Surface Vessels, which are capable of traveling up to 50 miles per hour on the water, as well as QinetiQ’s Banshee Whirlwind aerial drone flying at a speed of 200 miles per hour. The destroyer also shot at simulated maritime vessels, aircraft and mock cruise and ballistic missiles and simulated firing its Sea Viper surface-to-air missile system while the ship’s Wildcat helicopter engaged targets using its Martlet anti-ship missiles.
Duncan met its overall objectives of Sharpshooter by tracking and neutralizing five aerial targets and sinking two Hammerheads, reads the release.
